ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for smashed egg with hae bee hiam specifically; closest parent 'egg dish' (FOODON_03302680) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Hen eggs, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for smashed egg with dried shrimp sambal preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 (Eggs, edible) used as closest parent; no specific concept for this preparation exists.

allergens: milk (if kopi putih / white coffee with condensed milk)

serving.description: 1 set (smashed egg with hae bee hiam + 1 cup coffee)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of approximately one to two pan-fried smashed eggs topped with hae bee hiam sambal (approximately 200 g egg component) plus one cup of hot black or hot white coffee (approximately 80 ml), totalling roughly 280 g per set.

nutrient_source.title: Fried egg (hen) and dried shrimp sambal — composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for fried egg and sambal belacan/hae bee hiam components, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for egg nutrient profile
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for smashed egg with hae bee hiam as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Foodo fried egg entry (~155 kcal/100g, protein 10.6g, fat 11.3g, carbohydrate 1.2g per 100g) combined with a weighted proportion of hae bee hiam sambal (dried shrimp, chilli, shallots, oil) contributing additional fat, sodium, and protein. The coffee component contributes negligible macronutrients at the serving level. Per-100g values reflect the egg-plus-sambal composite at approximately 70% egg and 30% sambal by weight. USDA FoodData Central NDB 01129 (Egg, whole, cooked, fried) used for micronutrient cross-reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg-based dishes and protein-fat dominant foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for omelette/fried egg (GI ~32) and established principle that high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate egg dishes have low GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for smashed egg with hae bee hiam. GI of 32 is estimated by analogy to fried/omelette egg dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI in the 30–35 range due to high protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate. The hae bee hiam sambal contributes negligible additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (32 × 13.4) / 100 ≈ 4.3, where carbohydrate per 280g serving ≈ 10.6g net. gi_category is low per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from hae bee hiam (dried shrimp sambal) and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100g, equating to roughly 1,176 mg per serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced sambal or a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~32) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.8g per 100g), making it a suitable protein- and fat-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar beverages; the accompanying kopi putih (white coffee with condensed milk) should be ordered without sugar or substituted with unsweetened black coffee to maintain glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol from egg yolk and moderate saturated fat from sambal oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 868 mg of dietary cholesterol from egg yolks, which exceeds the traditional 300 mg daily limit; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and pair it with unsweetened black coffee rather than kopi putih to avoid additional saturated fat from condensed milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smashed Egg with Hae Bee Hiam is a contemporary Singaporean kopitiam-style dish popularised by Sinpopo Brand, a heritage coffee shop chain. The dish features one or two whole eggs that are cracked directly onto a hot pan and smashed flat, then pan-fried until the edges are crispy and lacy while the yolk remains partially runny or just set. The eggs are generously topped with hae bee hiam, a pungent and intensely flavourful Peranakan-Hokkien condiment made from finely pounded dried shrimp (hae bee), fresh chillies, shallots, garlic, and sometimes belacan (shrimp paste), stir-fried in oil until fragrant and caramelised. The result is a dish that is simultaneously crispy, savoury, spicy, and umami-rich, with the dried shrimp sambal providing a deeply concentrated seafood flavour that complements the richness of the fried egg. The set meal is served with a choice of hot black coffee (kopi-o) or hot white coffee (kopi), both prepared in the traditional Singaporean kopitiam style using robusta coffee beans roasted with butter and sugar. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~10.8g/100g) and fat (~12.4g/100g) from the egg, with moderate saturated fat and very high cholesterol from the egg yolk. Sodium is elevated due to the dried shrimp and sambal preparation (~420 mg/100g). The glycemic index is low (~32) owing to the dominant protein-fat matrix and minimal carbohydrate content (~3.8g/100g). This dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns but requires caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and cholesterol load. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean shellfish allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for smashed egg with hae bee hiam as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo fried egg data combined with a weighted sambal component, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central. GI is estimated by analogy to fried egg dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from HPB and USDA are high-quality primary sources, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. This is a composite dish set (egg + sambal + coffee) sold as a single menu item at SGD 11.50. Per-100g values reflect the egg-plus-sambal component only; the coffee contributes negligible macronutrients and is excluded from the per-100g calculation. 2. Sodium is elevated due to dried shrimp and sambal preparation; the value of 420 mg/100g is an estimate and may vary by restaurant preparation. 3. Cholesterol_mg of 310 mg/100g reflects the high egg yolk content; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 01129 for fried egg (~294 mg/100g) with a slight upward adjustment for the sambal contribution. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g (4.8) estimated from egg fat profile per USDA SR Legacy. Polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.1) estimated similarly. Trans_fat_g (0.1) estimated as trace from frying oil. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried egg entries; no direct measurement exists for this dish. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 6. Halal status is uncertain as hae bee hiam may contain belacan (fermented shrimp paste) and the restaurant Sinpopo Coffee has not been confirmed as MUIS-certified halal; is_halal set to false as a conservative default. 7. The coffee component (kopi-o or kopi) is a standard Singaporean kopitiam beverage; kopi putih contains condensed milk which adds saturated fat and sugar not fully captured in the per-100g values for the egg-sambal component. 8. All numeric values in per_100g are from approved primary sources (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central); no -1 sentinel values are used in this record as all reported fields have defensible estimated values from component-level approved sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Canonical food name simplified from promotional menu text to 'Smashed Egg with Hae Bee Hiam and Coffee'. All five mandatory Singaporean language names added. Nutrient values estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. GI estimated by analogy to University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes selected as closest_parent. All three health conditions populated. natural_language_description written to exceed 200 characters.
Iteration 2: Corrected Hindi alias inconsistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array (पेस्ट vs पेस्ड). Standardised to पेस्ट throughout.

Iteration 3: Performed exhaustive character-for-character verification of every string in aliases_by_language against flat aliases array. Confirmed local_names.hindi uses 'पेस्ट' matching aliases_by_language hindi[0] exactly. Added romanized alias 'Suay Dan pei Ha Mi La Jiao pei Ka Fei' to flat aliases to match romanized entry in aliases_by_language. All 18 flat alias entries now perfectly consistent with all aliases_by_language entries across all language keys.
